section.title: Fubini's theorem and change of variables

subsection.title: Fubini's theorem: statement
subsection.p:
If f is continuous on a rectangle R=[a,b]\times[c,d], then
\boxed{ \iint_R f(x,y)\,dA = \int_a^b\int_c^d f(x,y)\,dy\,dx = \int_c^d\int_a^b f(x,y)\,dx\,dy. }

subsection.title: Fubini: hypotheses
subsection.p: For the version used most often in this book:

md.mrow: 1. \amp \text{The region is a rectangle, box, or a region described by clean bounds.}
2. \amp \text{The function is continuous, or at least integrable in a safe sense.}

subsection.title: Fubini: proof idea
subsection.p: A double integral is a limit of sums over small rectangles: \sum f(x_i^*,y_j^*)\,\Delta x\,\Delta y.

Group the same sum by columns:
\sum_i \left( \sum_j f(x_i^*,y_j^*)\,\Delta y \right) \Delta x.
The inner sum becomes an integral in y. The outer sum becomes an integral in x.
Group by rows instead, and the order reverses.

subsection.title: Fubini: what can fail
subsection.p: For ordinary continuous functions on bounded regions, Fubini is safe.
For improper integrals, changing the order can change the value if the integral is not absolutely convergent.
The safe rule is:
Without absolute convergence, be careful.

subsection.title: Change of variables: statement
subsection.p:
For a C^1 coordinate map T(u,v)=(x(u,v),y(u,v)), the change of variables formula is
\boxed{ \iint_R f(x,y)\,dA = \iint_{\widetilde R} f(T(u,v)) \left| \det DT(u,v) \right| \,du\,dv. }
The ordinary area formula uses |\det DT|.

subsection.title: Change of variables: hypotheses
subsection.p: The standard version assumes:

md.mrow: 2. \amp T\text{ is one-to-one on the region, except possibly on boundary pieces.}
3. \amp \det DT\ne0\text{ in the interior, except possibly on harmless boundary pieces.}

subsection.title: Change of variables: proof idea
subsection.p:
Near a point (u,v), the map T is almost linear:
T(u+\Delta u,v+\Delta v) \approx T(u,v)+DT \begin{pmatrix} \Delta u\\ \Delta v \end{pmatrix}.
A tiny rectangle in the uv-plane becomes almost a parallelogram in the xy-plane.
Add over many tiny rectangles and pass to the limit.

md.mrow: 2. \amp \text{Determinant as area or volume scale factor.}

subsection.title: Change of variables: what can fail
subsection.p: If the map is not one-to-one, the integral may count points more than once.
Example: polar coordinates with 0\le \theta\le4\pi trace a full disk twice.
If the Jacobian vanishes on a whole region, the map may collapse area.
If the orientation matters, do not replace \det DT by |\det DT|. The sign is part of the form.

subsection.title: Moral
subsection.p: Fubini explains how to add by slices. Change of variables explains how small pieces change size and orientation under a new coordinate language.
